global.greeting: Good morning, Tim. Ask me anything about this briefing — the liquidity picture, the numbers behind it, how far you can trust them, or the recommendation. You can also click any figure, chart, or card to ask about it.

default.a: I can speak to anything in this briefing — the liquidity watch, the evidence and trend, the trajectory, data trust, the members, or the recommended plan. Try one of these:
followups: Walk me through the liquidity issue

questions.a: A strong, well-run institution with one thing to manage. Assets are $1.53B, up 8.2% year over year; net income is $9.4M and 6.8% ahead of plan; capital is healthy at a 7.50% net worth ratio; and membership is up 3.1% to 52,488. The one item that needs a decision is liquidity — loan demand is outpacing deposits and loan-to-share has reached 84%.
followups: Walk me through the liquidity issue

questions.q: Walk me through the liquidity issue
questions.a: Net loans grew 11.4% year to date while shares grew just 4.1%. That gap has pushed loan-to-share to 84%, the highest in five years, and on the current path it crosses the 85% board ceiling in September and dips below your internal liquidity target by late Q4. It's board-level and forward-looking, but manageable with the recommended moves.
followups: What happens if we don't act?

questions.a: Yes. Every figure was validated overnight and reconciled at 6:04 AM ET — 6 of 6 sources loaded. One exception was caught: the Verafin fraud feed loaded 41 minutes late, so one dependent figure was held for reconciliation and kept out of your headline numbers. NCUA exam readiness sits at 88, and any figure can be traced to source on demand.

Pair a targeted deposit campaign to the DMV core and Senate-alumni segments with a standby funding-line review. The campaign lifts shares quickly — about +$85M — and closes most of the gap by year end; the funding line holds a buffer through Q1. Both are sized for the September board.

The Verafin third-party fraud feed loaded 41 minutes past its window overnight. The trust layer caught it, held the single dependent figure for reconciliation, and kept it out of your headline numbers — so nothing reached the board unvalidated. This is exactly the kind of silent failure that used to surface only at month-end.
Opening the source-to-screen trace — follow any figure from the Symitar core through ETL and the warehouse to this brief.

suggested: Why is this a watch, not critical?

questions.a: Loan-to-share is net loans divided by member shares. Net loans grew 11.4% this year while shares grew only 4.1%, so the ratio climbed to 84% — its highest in five years. It's the single number that captures the liquidity pressure.
followups: What happens if we don't act?

questions.a: Loan demand is running nearly three times faster than deposit growth — up 11.4% year to date against 4.1% share growth. Consumer lending through MeridianLink is the largest contributor, with a $312M 90-day pipeline still incoming.
followups: Why is this a watch, not critical?

questions.a: It's the 90-day loan origination pipeline from MeridianLink — $312M of demand already in motion. It matters because that incoming volume keeps pushing loan-to-share higher before any deposit response can catch up.
followups: What happens if we don't act?

questions.q: Why is this a watch, not critical?
questions.a: It's a Watch, not a crisis — capital is healthy and the trend is manageable if addressed now. But it's genuinely board-level: on the current path liquidity tightens below your internal target by late Q4, so the decision belongs on the September board agenda, not later.
followups: What happens if we don't act?

questions.a: One governed number from three systems: net loans of $1.29B from Symitar, member shares of $1.54B from the General Ledger, and the $312M pipeline from MeridianLink. Reconciled overnight, they resolve to a single loan-to-share ratio of 84% — not three figures that disagree. Confidence is 94%.
$1.29B of net loans from the Symitar core, up 11.4% year to date. That's the numerator of the loan-to-share ratio and the side that's been growing fastest.
$1.54B of member shares from the General Ledger, up only 4.1% year to date. Deposits simply haven't kept pace with lending — that's the gap the recommended deposit campaign is designed to close.

Loan-to-share has climbed steadily from 72% in 2022 to 84% today — the highest in five years. It's a structural drift, not a one-month spike, which is why it warrants a deliberate deposit strategy rather than wait-and-see.
Yes — every figure carries a documented path: Symitar to ETL to Greenplum to Brief. You can show that chain to the board or an NCUA examiner on demand.

suggested: When does it cross the ceiling?
What happens if we don't act?

questions.q: What happens if we don't act?
questions.a: Left unmanaged, loan-to-share rises above the 85% board ceiling in September and reaches about 89% by December — and liquidity dips below your internal target by late Q4. This is the base case with no intervention; it's why the recommendation is sized for the September board.
followups: When does it cross the ceiling?

questions.q: When does it cross the 85% ceiling?
questions.a: In September, on the current trajectory. That's the board's internal ceiling, which is why the decision needs to reach the September board meeting rather than waiting for year-end.
followups: What happens if we don't act?

questions.a: 91% confidence. It's a base-case model built on Symitar balances, the MeridianLink pipeline, and current growth rates, with no intervention assumed. The interventions on the recommendation slide are modeled against this same baseline.
followups: What happens if we don't act?

questions.a: Yes — 6 of 6 sources loaded and reconciled at 6:04 AM ET, including Symitar, MeridianLink, and Greenplum. The overnight load completed cleanly aside from one caught exception.

The Verafin fraud feed loaded 41 minutes late. The trust layer caught it, held the one dependent figure for reconciliation, and kept it out of your headline numbers — nothing reached the board unvalidated. This is the silent failure that used to surface only when a month-end number looked off.

NCUA exam readiness is 88 out of 100 — a strong posture, with the next exam window in Q3 2026. Because every figure is traceable to source, you can answer an examiner's request on demand rather than reconstructing it by hand.

suggested: How does the deposit plan help members?

questions.q: How does the deposit plan help members?
questions.a: Deposits aren't abstractions — they're the members' savings. The recommended campaign targets the DMV core and the growing national base of Senate alumni, so the liquidity fix is also a membership-deepening move: it grows the very relationships USSFCU was built to serve.

Three: 2025 CEO of the Year from the MD|DC Credit Union Association, a Top Workplaces recognition, and a Bauer 5-Star rating. Together they signal a member-first institution that's also financially sound — the credibility behind the plan you'll take to the board.
followups: How does the deposit plan help members?

questions.q: Tell me about the deposit campaign
questions.a: Option 1, the recommended path: a promotional certificate to the DMV core and Senate-alumni segments to lift shares quickly. Timeline 2 to 4 weeks, low cost, high impact — about +$85M in shares. It's the fastest, cheapest way to close most of the gap.

questions.q: Tell me about the standby funding line
questions.a: Option 2: establish a contingent FHLB or corporate line for standby liquidity through Q1. Timeline 4 to 6 weeks, low standby fee, medium impact — it's a buffer rather than a cure, which is why it pairs so well with the deposit campaign.

Option 3: slow select loan categories or sell participations to rebalance the ratio. Timeline 6 to 8 weeks, medium cost, and it carries a real trade-off — foregone yield. It works, but it's the least attractive because it constrains the lending franchise that's performing well.

It's the fastest, lowest-cost, highest-impact move — about +$85M in shares in 2 to 4 weeks — and it grows member deposits rather than constraining lending. It closes most of the gap on its own; pairing it with the standby line just adds a buffer.

The options aren't mutually exclusive. The recommended plan pairs the deposit campaign with the standby funding line — close the gap now with deposits, hold a buffer through Q1 with the line. That's the two-move recommendation you'd take to the board.

Option 1 (deposit campaign) is fast, cheap, high impact — the recommended core. Option 2 (standby funding line) is a low-cost buffer, medium impact. Option 3 (loan pacing and participations) is slower, costlier, and gives up yield. The plan uses 1 and 2, and holds 3 in reserve.

suggested: What if the board doesn't approve?

questions.q: Tell me about the deposit campaign approval
questions.a: Step 1: approve the targeted deposit campaign to the DMV core and Senate-alumni segments. Owners are the CFO (Ricardo Pineres), Marketing, and Lending, with a timeframe of before month-end. It's the move that closes most of the liquidity gap.

questions.q: Tell me about the funding-line review
questions.a: Step 2: commission the funding-line review and size a standby FHLB or corporate line. Owners are the CFO (Ricardo Pineres) and Treasury, within 5 business days. It secures the contingent buffer behind the deposit campaign.

questions.q: Tell me about the board briefing
questions.a: Step 3: brief the Board on the liquidity plan and the two-move recommendation for approval. Owners are the CEO (you) and the CFO, at the September board meeting. That's where the plan gets the mandate to execute.
followups: What if the board doesn't approve?

questions.a: The CFO (Ricardo Pineres) anchors all three: he leads the deposit campaign with Marketing and Lending, the funding-line review with Treasury, and co-presents to the board with you. Clear single-threaded ownership, with the board decision resting with the two of you.
followups: What if the board doesn't approve?

questions.a: Deposit campaign approval before month-end; funding-line review within 5 business days; board briefing at the September meeting. Sequenced so the two operational moves are underway before the board formally approves the plan.
followups: What if the board doesn't approve?

questions.a: I can assemble the board memo from this briefing — the liquidity watch, the evidence and trajectory, the two-move recommendation, and the lineage appendix behind every figure. In the full build this generates the document; here it's staged and ready to present.

questions.q: What if the board doesn't approve?
questions.a: If the board holds, Option 3 — loan pacing and participations — is the fallback: it rebalances the ratio without new deposits, at the cost of some yield. But the deposit campaign and standby line are low-risk enough that a hold mainly costs time, and time is the one thing the late-Q4 trajectory doesn't give you.
followups: What happens if we don't act?
